So I spoke to Jacob, who's a managing director here, just to get his insights, and he kind of put it pretty simply, which is, the assumption is that everything that we're pruning from the blog is either, is low quality content, either because it's thin, or it's irrelevant, or it just never got any traffic.
And it's better off being removed.
So the assumption is that everything that remains is higher quality content that we actually want people to visit.
And it doesn't have to be super high performance.
It could be like thought leadership posts that some of your team members have written about something that's super specific, but it's still valuable to have on the blog.
So his idea was to create a page in our Looker dashboard, so it would just be a brand new page, with one chart that would be like tracking the performance of blog posts that remain on the website.

So we would like after we prune all of the blog posts that we don't want to be on there, we would then set this chart up, and we would be able to track if the traffic performance is improving, like over time.

More traffic to these other blogs, they are improving in search rankings.

Pilot.

The reason, I kind of took a, I did a little digging, and I saw that Google's September core update finished rolling out, like, at the end of September, the start of October.

I think it was a spam, another spam update.
